summary.category: People as Resource and Human Capital Formation
points: 'People as Resource' refers to the working population in terms of their existing productive skills and abilities, emphasizing population as an asset rather than a liability for the economy.

Human capital is the stock of skill and productive knowledge embodied in people; human capital formation occurs when investment in education, training, and medical care develops this resource, adding to the country's productive power.
Investment in human capital yields returns through higher incomes and productivity, and human capital is superior to other resources because it can make use of land and capital.

summary.category: Economic Activities and Gender Division of Labour
points: Economic activities are classified into three sectors: primary (agriculture, mining), secondary (manufacturing), and tertiary (services); they are divided into market activities (for pay/profit) and non-market activities (self-consumption).

Women's household work is not recognized in National Income; women receive payment only when entering the labour market, with earnings determined by education and skill—women with high education are paid at par with men.

summary.category: Quality of Population: Education and Health
points: Quality of population depends on literacy rate, health (life expectancy), and skill formation; these factors decide a country's growth rate. India's literacy rate increased from 18% in 1951 to 85% in 2018, with gaps between males and females (16.1%) and urban and rural areas (14.2%).
Health indicators show improvement with life expectancy rising to 67.2 years (2021) and Infant Mortality Rate dropping from 147 in 1951 to 28 in 2020.

Government initiatives like Samagra Shiksha and Mid-day Meal Scheme promote education, while national health policies focus on accessible healthcare with special attention to underprivileged segments.

Unemployment exists when willing workers cannot find jobs at going wages. Rural areas face seasonal unemployment (during non-agricultural months) and disguised unemployment (where extra workers don't add to productivity).
Urban areas face educated unemployment where degree-holders cannot find jobs, creating a paradoxical situation of surplus in some categories and shortages in others.
Unemployment leads to wastage of manpower, economic overload, decline in quality of life, and adverse impact on overall economic growth.

The case studies of Sakal (who invested in education and secured a good job) and Vilas (who lacked education and remained in low-income work) demonstrate that education and health investments yield high returns.
The virtuous cycle shows educated parents invest more in children's education and health; the vicious cycle shows disadvantaged parents keep children in similarly disadvantaged states.

The story of a village illustrates how rising human capital through education, skill training, and local initiatives can transform a simple village into a place with diverse economic activities and prosperity.
Countries like Japan succeeded by investing in human capital despite lacking natural resources, showing that people's efficiency and technology drive development.
